metadata.title: Post-crisis Political Normalisation? The 2018 Presidential Elections in the Republic of Cyprus

abstract: This article takes stock of the most recent presidential election in the Republic of Cyprus and connects it to broader currents in postcrisis European political settings. Although the elections took place against a backdrop of improving economic prospects, the crisis has left its political mark in a number of areas we identify that include a growing political fragmentation, the rise of the far right and increased voter abstention. The empirical analysis focuses on the media campaign and draws on a topic-modelling approach to identify and contrast emphasis given to policy issues over the two electoral rounds. The policy themes identified clustered around two dominant dimensions of political conflict: the Cyprus conflict and the economy.

An important aspect of the 2018 election is that it was the first presidential election to take place after the traumatic event of Cyprus' bailout during the eurozone crisis. The Cypriot bailout was particularly harsh involving, among other things, the bailing-in of depositors of two major banks. This triggered an unprecedented public outcry. Yet, while the ensuing economic downturn was pronounced, by 2018 Cyprus had already formally exited supervision under the Troika institutions (the IMF, ECB and European Commission) and the prospects for the economy were distinctly positive. Having 'normalised' aspects of its financial situation, could the same be said of its politics? In one area this was no doubt the case: the Cyprus problem re-established its primacy as the main conflict structuring political competition. In this narrow sense, the 2018 elections seem to have taken the course of a rather usual electoral contest. Nonetheless, the turbulent economic times of the recent past seem to have left traces that persist.

In addressing the question of whether politics has normalised or whether the crisis has generated some structural changes, we begin by situating Cyprus against a broader backdrop of crisis in European political systems, and in particular the so-called bailout countries of Southern Europe. We then provide some further background on the Cypriot political context in the run up to the presidential election of 2018 before moving on to the analysis of the campaign. Here we follow two tracks. First, we present the candidates and their main positions. The core empirical analysis of the media campaign then follows, in which we use a topic-modelling approach to analyse campaign coverage of the main news outlets in the RoC. We close with the results and a broader discussion about post-crisis politics in the island.

sections.header_raw: The crisis and its political aftershocks
sections.header_norm: the crisis and its political aftershocks
paragraphs: In this section we attempt to situate the Cypriot political context against the backdrop of broader political currents afflicting European countries. The Cypriot ideological space is structured upon two well-known dimensions of political competition that pervade most European political systems (Triga 2017). According to this two-dimensional understanding of political conflict in most European countries, the first axis concerns conflict over how the economy is regulated and pits those in favour of more state regulation against proponents of liberal markets. This is frequently referred to as the economic left/right axis.

The second dimension of conflict tends to agglomerate a mix of issues areas related to identity, culture and social mores. Depending on the research programme followed, a number of labels have emerged to describe this second dimension: libertarian vs. authoritarian (Kitschelt & Rehm 2015); cultural integration vs. cultural demarcation (Kriesi et al. 2006) and GAL/TAN (Hooghe, Marks & Wilson 2002). The specific bundle of issues driving the second dimension varies across European systems. In the Cypriot case, the most important issue belonging to this second non-economic dimension is the so-called Cyprus problem.

Traditionally, the Cyprus problem (further elaborated below) has constituted the super issue of political conflict. That dominance may have been (temporarily) punctuated by the euro crisis when Cyprus was one of the rescued eurozone members. Evidently, the economic dimension took centre stage at the peak of the crisis when Cyprus was bailed out and underwent economic supervision by the Troika. At this point, the economic dimension of conflict may have even 'side-lined' the traditional super issue (Katsourides 2014). In their study of bailout countries, specifically the case of Greece, Katsanidou and Otjes (2016) present evidence of how the issue of political dimensionality is interpreted differently between European countries. Whereas for many northern European countries the issue of Europe is interpreted through what Kriesi et al. (2006) call the 'cultural' dimension of conflict, for many of the bailout countries Europe is mediated through the economic dimension. Cyprus follows, in this sense, patterns of conflict that have been common among bailout countries. In one notable aspect, the rise of far right parties, Cyprus has followed in the footsteps of Greece with the rise of a similar far right party to its Golden Dawn. Indeed, the Cypriot variant, ELAM (Εθνικό Λαϊκό Μέτωπο -National Popular Front), closely cooperates with its 'brother' party in Greece.

There is a growing literature on the effects of the crisis on political systems across Europe-a debate that is obviously pertinent to the Cypriot case. Scholars points out that political changes commonly accompany financial crises in general (Funke, Schularick & Trebesch 2016). The 'Great Recession' (Kriesi 2012) of recent years was particularly disruptive to a number of European countries, triggering what for some is a democratic crisis of representation (Bosco & Verney 2016;Calvo, Chang & Hellwig 2017;Halikiopoulou & Vasilopoulou 2018;Morlino & Quaranta 2016).

The political crisis is more apparent in Southern Europe (especially the bailout countries) which implemented harsh austerity programmes, in many cases under the supervision of the Troika. The sudden drop in income, the growing unemployment and a generalised climate of uncertainty were decisive in breaking citizens' confidence and trust in their political institutions and actors (Halikiopoulou & Vasilopoulou 2018;Bosco & Verney 2016). Changes were immediately felt in the party system with increased polarisation, the emergence of populist and extreme parties, and growing instability among the 'old' parties (Calvo, Chang & Hellwig 2017;Hernández & Kriesi 2016;Freire & Lisi 2016;Conti, Hutter & Nanou 2018;Chiaramonte & Emanuele 2017).

A common list of democratic ailments in the post-crisis era includes growing voter abstention and apathy, electoral volatility, party de-alignment, voter disillusionment, distrust and dissatisfaction with the overall political system (Kitschelt & Rehm 2015;Verney & Bosco 2013;Kriesi & Pappas 2015;Torcal 2014;Hernández & Kriesi 2016;Magalhães 2014). These common features and the rise of the far right in a climate of public discontent have all been noted in the Cyprus case (Christophorou 2012;Katsourides 2014;Charalambous, Papageorgiou & Pegasiou 2015;Triga 2017).

The general political consequences of the financial crisis have been aptly captured by Funke, Schularick and Trebesch (2016) who, in their longitudinal study of political aftershocks following financial crises across 20 countries, point to systemic phenomena that are observable five years after an economy recovers from the crisis. These include the rise of extremist and in particular, far-right or populist parties, a drop in the vote share of governing coalitions and an increase in party fragmentation. Such post-crisis trends are more evident in the wake of financial crises rather than after normal recessions or other type of disasters or crises (Funke, Schularick & Trebesch 2016, p. 228). Thus, a question pervading analysis of the most recent elections in Cyprus is the extent to which such changes are becoming a more embedded feature of the political system.

paragraphs: When Anastasiades won the 2013 presidential election, he was immediately confronted with negotiating a rescue package for the Cypriot economy with his Eurozone partners. The eventual package included the bailing in of depositors with over 100,000 Euros in two of the afflicted banks and the hitherto unprecedented imposition of capital controls in a Eurozone country. However, while it is the case that the Anastasiades presidency was stigmatised by the implementation of some of the harshest austerity measures in the EU, it was also within his five-year term that the recovery of the economy occurred. The crowning achievement was Cyprus' formal exit in March 2016 from the supervision by the Troika of international lenders (the International Monetary Fund, European Commission and European Central Bank). This has made it much easier to attribute responsibility for the crisis to Dimitris Christofias, President for the period of 2008-13, and his party, AKEL.

The economic outlook for Cyprus on the eve of the presidential elections could be said to have been distinctly positive, as shown in Figure 1. The Troika supervision had been formally terminated in 2016 and since then growth had returned to an estimated 3.9 per cent for the last trimester of 2017. Even unemployment, which remains stubbornly high, had continued to drop from its 16 per cent peak at the height of the crisis in 2013. Figure 1 offers two central messages which informed aspects of the campaign narrative. First, the collapse of the economy coincided with the AKEL-backed Christofias presidency and second, the remarkable economic recovery was implemented under the leadership of Anastasiades. This provided the incumbent with a powerful argument regarding his administration's competent economic management. Nonetheless, despite the improving economic prospects, major structural problems remained. Among the most important of these outstanding issues is the health of the banking sector and, in particular, the weight of the non-performing loans on the Cypriot economy. This is a direct legacy of the eurozone crisis, with Cyprus in third position globally (Ukraine is first) in terms of the IMF's 2017 ranking of countries' ratio of non-performing loans compared to total gross loans. In 2017 Cyprus had a staggering ratio of 40.2%, which is close to Greece's 45.6%the highest in the Eurozoneand nearly three times higher than the next Eurozone country, Italy (14.2%) (IMF 2017).

paragraphs: Cyprus is internationally known for a longstanding conflict over its divided status and the series of unsuccessful efforts over the years to achieve a solution. The Cyprus problem is understood as the long-standing de facto partition of the island into a southern Greek Cypriot and a northern Turkish Cypriot part. The partition followed the Turkish invasion of the northern part of the island in 1974, in response to a coup in Cyprus organised by the Junta in Greece. Since then the southern part, the RoC, has been the only internationally recognised state. The Cyprus problem is undoubtedly the dominant political cleavage in Cypriot politics (Christophorou 2007). Not surprisingly, the issue tends to attract more political contestation with every new effort towards a solution. Furthermore, given the President's unique role in any proposed negotiation, most of the political attention is directed to the President during such junctures.

During his tenure as President, Anastasiades was instrumental in launching a major initiative. In February 2014 a Joint Declaration was signed by the leaders of the two communities (Greek and Turkish Cypriot) stating the goal of the creation of a bi-zonal, bi-communal federation with a single sovereignty. This paved the way for UN-backed negotiations between the two leaders. In the run up to the 2016 parliamentary elections in the RoC, progress seemed to have been made with a high degree of consensus on many issue fronts. However, contentious issues remained. Among these the most salient were territory and property exchanges as well as that of security and the withdrawal of the military forces of the three guarantor powers, namely Turkey, Greece and the UK.

In November 2016, the leaders of the two communities took part in several rounds of meetings in Switzerland. Thereafter, Greece and Turkey also joined talks in January 2017. However, the end result was unsuccessful, with the last part of the negotiations acrimoniously breaking down in Crans-Montana (Switzerland) on 6-7 July 2017, with each side blaming the other. The reasons for this last 'failure' and, in particular, the way President Anastasiades dealt with the negotiations, would became a central issue of the campaign and of conflict among the parties. The communist AKEL accused President Anastasiades of not having any real intention of advancing the negotiations, given the presidential elections looming on the horizon. The rest of the parties charged Anastasiades with being too consensual and ready to yield to Turkey's pressure. Herein lies one of the major lines of conflict on the Cyprus issue: on the one side, those in favour (at least in principle) of pursuing negotiations towards a bicommunal, bizonal federation who include the President, his party, DISY (Δημοκρατικός Συναγερμός -Democratic Rally) and AKEL-and those against such arrangement on the other side. The latter view incorporates various positions that range from the advocacy of a unitary state with a minority status for the Turkish Cypriots to unification with Greece.

sections.paper_id: Triga et al. - 2019 - Post-crisis Political Normalisation The 2018 Presidential Elections in the Republic of Cyprus
paragraphs: One notable feature of the 2018 presidential election was a distinct climate of polarisation which was manifested by the absence of traditional party alliances. As noted above, the presidential elections in the RoC are by far the most salient electoral contest, given the powerful role exercised by the President as both head of government and head of state. This does not mean parties are weak in the Cypriot political system. Indeed, political parties are central to all matters of political life and play a core function in the recruitment of the political elite (Katsourides 2013), not to mention as a vehicle for presidential ambitions. In recent years, parties have played a growing role as a check on presidential authority. This was most evident when AKEL secured the presidency for the first time under Christofias. During the latter's ultimately unpopular tenure, the parties were successful in exercising mounting pressure on the President through the mobilisation of public opinion and negative media as well as through parliamentary procedures. This was unprecedented, given the high degree of deference shown to the office of President by the mainstream media and the legislature's relative weakness in checking presidential powers (Ker-Lindsay 2006).

In view of the fact that it is very hard for a single party or candidate to cross the 50 per cent threshold to be elected, at least in the first round, the system pushes parties towards forging electoral alliances around common candidates. Despite the conflictual positions adopted by parties during such electoral contests, this forging of alliances has been a notable feature of Cypriot presidential elections (Christophorou 2008). The parties, in this way, channel their political antagonism via alliances in support of particular candidates. Thus far, no President has been elected without the support of another party. That support can be quite fragile and collapse after the election, as was the case for Anastasiades' first presidency, when the two parties that supported him in 2013, namely EVROKO (Ευρωπαϊκό Κόμμα -European Party) and DIKO (Δημοκρατικό Κόμμα -Democratic Party) broke up the alliance at different instances during the last five years. Thus, although a consensual approach among political parties is necessary, the fact that it occurs tends to serve specific political strategies rather than programmatic congruence-all of which make the alliances fragile and inconsistent (Christophorou 2008;Ker-Lindsay 2008).

In the 2018 election, we observe an almost unprecedented situation. The tradition of forging alliances between parties in support of the main candidates is broken and is accompanied by a higher number of significant candidates (five). Table 1 presents the elected presidents of the RoC beginning with the elections in 1978, the first following the Turkish invasion and occupation of the northern part of the island. The 1978 event was rather unique after all the contesting candidates resigned. The table also shows the elected presidents' party affiliation and their support from other parties (some of which participated in the formation of the respective governments), as well as the final electoral result.

As can be seen from Table 1, since 1983 the President has usually been supported by a plurality of parties. Indeed, the median number supporting the elected President over the past 35 years is four parties. Even independent Presidents, i.e. those without an official partisan membership, were supported by more than one party. The 2018 election was unprecedented in this regard as no other party supported Anastasiades apart from his own DISY. In addition, if we look at the number of candidates who received a vote share over 2 per cent, the median before 2013 is three candidates. The 2018 election also breaks ranks in this regard and, alongside, the contested 1998 elections, saw five candidates emerge with a significant vote share. This is connected to a growing fragmentation of the party system-a trend that could already be detected in the 2016 parliamentary elections (Triga 2017).

paragraphs: The next two sections present an overall picture of the electoral campaign largely from a political communication perspective (McNair 2016). In this first section, our focus is on the main actors, i.e. the candidates themselves and their central message as well as their support among the parties. This will be followed by a second section examining the role of the media and the dominant topics on which they focused. We begin by introducing the five candidates as well as their overall presence during the long electoral campaign.

paragraphs: As already mentioned, the relatively high number of significant candidates was a distinctive feature of the 2018 election. Table 2 presents the candidates and their ideological orientation as well as the parties supporting the candidates and the former's vote share in the previous 2016 parliamentary elections. What is striking is that four of the five main candidates secured the support of just one party. The notable exception was Nikolas Papadopoulos whose candidacy, as can be seen in Table 2, was backed by four of the parties in parliament. Apart from his own DIKO, these were EDEK (Ενιαία Δημοκρατική Ένωση Κέντρου -Unified Democratic Union of the Centre), KOP (Κίνημα Οικολόγων Περιβαλλοντιστών -Ecologists Environmentalists Movement, usually known as the Greens) and Solidarity (Αλληλεγγύη). These four parties had received a cumulative vote share of 30 per cent in 2016. This made Papadopoulos a strong favourite to make it to the second round. Indeed, the combined vote share of the parties supporting him was virtually identical to the 2016 vote share of the largest party in Parliament, DISY, which was backing its former leader, Anastasiades. AKEL, with its 25.6 per cent share of the vote, supported Malas. The two remaining parties in Parliament each fielded their own candidate.

sections.paper_id: Triga et al. - 2019 - Post-crisis Political Normalisation The 2018 Presidential Elections in the Republic of Cyprus
paragraphs: The first candidate was the incumbent President of the Republic since 2013, Nicos Anastasiades (72 years old). A seasoned politician with a long career, Anastasiades had been leader of his centre right party DISY since 1997 until his election as President. Although officially announced late in the campaign, his candidacy was considered certain. His political career was marked by his support of the UN-brokered Annan Plan, which constitutes the most significant re-unification attempt for the divided island. The Annan Plan was overwhelmingly rejected by the Greek Cypriot community in the referendum of 2004. The other major event that has marked his career is the adoption of the EU/IMF rescue plan during the first days after his election in 2013. As noted above, by the 2018 election Anastasiades could claim to have successfully returned the economy to a growth path. Not surprisingly, Anastasiades' campaign narrative focused on the need to continue a cautious policy of reforming the economy along with the modernisation of the state. These objectives were reflected in his campaign slogan 'Stable Steps Ahead' (Σταθερά Βήματα Μπροστά). This message had proved effective in the previous parliamentary election of 2016 and was re-emphasised during the 2018 campaign to reinforce in the electorate's mind the need to avoid risks when it came to the management of the economy.

In terms of the Cyprus problem, Anastasiades presented himself as determined to seek a solution-and as being the only experienced politician capable of delivering one (Triga 2017). In the column 'Party Support', we include in parenthesis the party's vote share for the 2016 parliamentary elections (in percentage). (Katsourides 2018). This, however, also became the focal point of conflict with other candidates-especially Papadopoulos-and their supporting parties. According to some analysts, Papadopoulos adopted a more hard-line position on the issue to avoid defection from some of the more conservative wing in his party who could be easily seduced by the siren call of others (Katsourides 2018).

These objectives were reflected in his campaign slogan 'Stable Steps Ahead' (Σταθερά Βήματα Μπροστά). This message had proved effective in the previous parliamentary election of 2016 and was re-emphasised during the 2018 campaign to reinforce in the electorate's mind the need to avoid risks when it came to the management of the economy.

In terms of the Cyprus problem, Anastasiades presented himself as determined to seek a solution-and as being the only experienced politician capable of delivering one (Triga 2017). In the column 'Party Support', we include in parenthesis the party's vote share for the 2016 parliamentary elections (in percentage). (Katsourides 2018). This, however, also became the focal point of conflict with other candidates-especially Papadopoulos-and their supporting parties. According to some analysts, Papadopoulos adopted a more hard-line position on the issue to avoid defection from some of the more conservative wing in his party who could be easily seduced by the siren call of others (Katsourides 2018).

A seasoned politician with a long career, Anastasiades had been leader of his centre right party DISY since 1997 until his election as President. Although officially announced late in the campaign, his candidacy was considered certain.

sections.paper_id: Triga et al. - 2019 - Post-crisis Political Normalisation The 2018 Presidential Elections in the Republic of Cyprus
paragraphs: The independent candidate, Stavros Malas, had already been a candidate during the 2013 election where he lost in the second round to Anastasiades. A 50-year-old doctor, Malas had held a ministerial position (as Health Minister) in 2011-12 under the Christofias presidency. Nonetheless, Malas is not considered a 'professional' politician.

For the 2018 elections, he announced his independent candidacy in July 2017 and was supported by AKEL (after an internal party vote). Malas presented his programme in September 2017 as well as his campaign slogan: 'Tomorrow Unites Us' (Μας ενώνει το αύριο). This was targeted at moderate voters belonging mostly to the centre left and the left. Malas tried to transmit a message of hope and a new start, not only for the country but also for AKEL, the party that supported him. In relation to the latter, Malas' major problem was to disassociate his candidacy from the previous AKEL presidency under Dimitris Christofias, who was widely blamed for the situation leading to the painful rescue of Cyprus in 2013. For this reason, both Malas and AKEL kept describing his candidacy as independent.

Malas employed two lines of argumentation. The first was centred on an antiausterity rhetoric with an emphasis on social issues and policies that sustain social welfare (health, education, social justice) and the support of socially sensitive groups of society. The second main line of argumentation concerned the Cyprus problem. Malas supported the principle of a bi-zonal, bi-communal federation as a solution to the Cyprus problem. His critique of Anastasiades on this front mostly centred on the failed negotiations, for which he held the President responsible. His fiercest critique, however, was against Papadopoulos, whom he accused of adopting an irresponsible position that would lead to a de facto partition of the island. Finally, it is worth mentioning that Malas also maintained a stricter position against any possible collaboration with the far right ELAM.

sections.paper_id: Triga et al. - 2019 - Post-crisis Political Normalisation The 2018 Presidential Elections in the Republic of Cyprus
paragraphs: A favourite for making it to the second round run-off, Nikolas Papadopoulos, was the leader of the 'centrist' party of DIKO (Δημοκρατικό Κόμμα -Democratic Party). He is also the son of a former President, Tassos Papadopoulos. The latter was famous for his part in the final negotiation of the 2004 Annan Plan and his last-minute move to withdraw support and to campaign against the Plan in the referendum. Papadopoulos' candidacy for the presidency was announced in April 2017 with the support of his own party, DIKO, alongside the socialist EDEK, the Greens and the relatively new party of Solidarity (which had entered parliament in the 2016 elections). Solidarity's leader, Eleni Theocharous, was actually a former DISY Member of Parliament who had decided to create a new party that would adopt a harder and more nationalistic line on the Cyprus problem.

The main reason that these three parties endorsed Papadopoulos' candidacy was their opposition to the notion of a bi-zonal and bi-communal federation as a proclaimed solution for the Cyprus problem. This was the key issue on which Papadopoulos' campaign mobilised. Indeed, in his last public meeting, Papadopoulos called the elections 'a referendum on the Cypriot issue' (Fileleftheros 2018). The major electoral slogan adopted by Papadopoulos' campaign was 'Change, Credibility, Hope' (Αλλαγή, Αξιοπιστία, Ελπίδα), which was supposed to underscore his plan to bring real change through a new strategy. Papadopoulos employed a very polarising discourse against the two other main candidates, Anastasiades and Malas, especially in relation to the Cyprus issue. He also engaged with a populist rhetoric focused on addressing the needs of weak citizens (e.g. those with non-performing loans) and against corruption and scandals. To create a brand identity around his persona, his campaign logo used his first name 'NIKOLAS2018ʹ (ΝΙΚΟΛΑΣ2018).

sections.paper_id: Triga et al. - 2019 - Post-crisis Political Normalisation The 2018 Presidential Elections in the Republic of Cyprus
paragraphs: The fourth player, Giorgos Lillikas, announced his candidacy for the presidential elections in May 2017. Lillikas, a 57-year-old experienced politician (elected in 1996 and 2001) had been a minister of Foreign Affairs in Tassos Papadopoulos' government. An ex-AKEL deputy, he is the leader of Citizens' Alliance (Συμμαχία Πολιτών), a small, recently founded party which seeks to challenge the hegemony of the 'old' parties. Lillikas had also been a presidential candidate in 2013, when he came third. His major slogan for the 2018 campaign was 'Safe Choice -We are Changing Cyprus' (Ασφαλής Επιλογή -Αλλάζουμε την Κύπρο). This was an attempt to portray himself as representing a middle position between the hard line of Papadopoulos and the 'compromising' stands of Anastasiades and Malas. The notion of change referred to his intention to instil change in Cypriot society and 'liberate' it from the established parties.

The major issues promoted by Lillikas' campaign were the Cyprus problem, the amelioration of the everyday life of Cypriot citizens following the financial crisis and the challenge to the established and allegedly corrupt parties, namely DISY, DIKO, and AKEL. Regarding the Cyprus problem, Lillikas advanced the notion of direct democracy and put forward a proposal for a binding referendum to decide the type of solution for the future state of Cyprus. This proposal can be interpreted as undermining the bi-zonal, bi-communal federation solution (Zakos 2018). Amongst other vague promises, Lillikas proposed the creation of an organisation that would purchase the non-performing loans. He also promised a minimum pension of 733 Euros (compared to 415 Euros at the time of the election).

sections.paper_id: Triga et al. - 2019 - Post-crisis Political Normalisation The 2018 Presidential Elections in the Republic of Cyprus
paragraphs: The fifth candidate, Christos Christou (38 years old), is the leader of the far right ELAM. Formally founded in 2011, ELAM is the 'brother' party of the Greek Golden Dawn. Its vote share has risen in every election since its founding and it crossed the threshold for parliamentary representation in 2016 when it gained two seats (one of them held by Christou). The slogan of Christou's campaign was 'This land has voice' ('Τούτη η γη έχει φωνή') featured in a poster campaign which presented the island of Cyprus as unified. The use of such a slogan aimed at enforcing a national, patriotic sentiment of pride and confidence. In contrast to the previous elections, in which the party of ELAM did not have a media presence, in the 2018 campaign Christou received significant media attention-as did all the candidates. His party used alternative campaign actions such as food collections or donations to communities (e.g. a fire truck) or provision of financial aid to citizens in need (such as the payment of a fine on behalf of an elderly citizen etc.) (Christou 2017).

The main issue on which the ELAM candidate campaigned was opposition to a federation. Instead he supported a new, unitary Cyprus in which the Turkish community would be considered a minority. To this end, Christou demanded an end to the negotiations with the Turkish Cypriots and the pursuit of a different strategy based on the notion that Cyprus is currently under illegal occupation. Needless to say, this was infused with nationalistic discourse along the lines of the Greek neo-fascist party, Golden Dawn.

Other ideas, typical of far right parties, included opposition to the rights of the LGTB community and of immigrants, as well as to the right to abortion, accompanied by strong support for the Orthodox Church. The populist component included a vigorous critique of the corrupt political system and a centrist (or even leftist) position on economic issues-again a familiar line among far right parties whose main mobilisation is on the cultural dimension. Here the proposed policies involved reducing unemployment, fixing the non-performing loans issue and setting a minimum pension at 800 Euros.

sections.paper_id: Triga et al. - 2019 - Post-crisis Political Normalisation The 2018 Presidential Elections in the Republic of Cyprus
paragraphs: The five candidates participated in a televised debate on 22 January, which was broadcast live by all Cypriot TV stations. The debate did not bring up any new aspects since candidates repeated their respective narratives in a pretty structured model of discussion with no interaction among them but only the requirement for each to answer journalists' questions within a limited time. The main issues which came up in the debate were the Cyprus problem, the economy and energy policy. According to analysts' commentary on the debate (Iefimerida 2018), Anastasiades, Malas and Lillikas appeared to have made the best impression and seemed the most prepared (Paraschos 2018).

sections.paper_id: Triga et al. - 2019 - Post-crisis Political Normalisation The 2018 Presidential Elections in the Republic of Cyprus
paragraphs: We now turn to the core empirical section in this paper: an analysis of how the media reflected the campaign and in particular how different policy issue areas clustered and the relative emphasis given to the main issues. This is connected to our broader concerns in situating the dimensions of political conflict in Cyprus against a wider backdrop of post-crisis politics in European states. To achieve our empirical goals, we implement a topic-modelling approach, a technique used in computer science that has also gained some traction among political scientists for analysing political text (Mildenberger & Tingley 2017;Lucas et al. 2015;Grimmer & Stewart 2013). This approach allows us to analyse how the media reflected the main policy issues driving the campaign. Since our interest is in coverage of the elections by the mainstream media, our first step was to select the relevant media sources for collecting our corpus of media articles. Apart from the requirement that the news sources had an online presence, we tried to balance two criteria: (1) coverage, i.e. to include the most important media channels and (2) to maximise the ideological range of the news content. To meet the first criterion, we included the three most widely read news media (Sigmalive, Phileleftheros and Politis). Sigmalive is the most popular news site-indeed it is the second most visited website of all in Cyprus after the search engine google.com (based on the Alexa ranking). Phileleftheros and Politis are the second and third most visited news websites.

Two newspapers, the smaller circulation, Dialogos, and the more mainstream Politis can be characterised as being broadly favourable to a bizonal arrangement while the two others, Sigmalive and Phileftheros, are more rejectionist on this issue. We use these two labels, pro-bizonal vs. rejectionist, as a shorthand for the dummy variable created in the models.

The corpus of documents (i.e. relevant articles) was created after filtering non-relevant articles and applying a time filter to cover only the official campaign period (28 January-28 February 2018) and an additional week between the first and the second round (28 February 2018 to 4 March 2018). The final corpus consisted of 2,669 articles (see Figure 2 for the distribution of documents per source). After pre-processing the corpus, we mined the data using a topic-modelling approach, a popular technique for exploratory analysis of documents in computer science and increasingly used in the social sciences too. The aim is to detect the most relevant topics from a given corpus of data, which in our case consists of media articles related to the presidential campaign in Cyprus.

One of the most widely used techniques for conducting this type of analysis is a generative probabilistic model called LDA (Blei, Ng & Jordan 2003). At the risk of grossly oversimplifying, the technique allows us to see how words that co-occur form clusters of topics. Our specific interest was in identifying how the media reflected the main issues of political competition in terms of what the issues were, how they clustered and their relative importance. Topic modelling is ideally suited to this task and is increasingly used in the field of political science and political communication (Mildenberger & Tingley 2017;Lucas et al. 2015;Grimmer & Stewart 2013).

One increasingly popular variant of topic modelling, developed by social scientists, is Structural Topic Models (STM) (Roberts, Stewart & Airoldi 2016;Roberts et al. 2014). Part of the attraction of using the STM version is that it allows social scientists to incorporate contextual variables during the model fitting process. For our analysis, we had a number of contextual variables that were pertinent to our enquiry. These included the position of the media sources on the Cyprus problem for which we created a dummy variable; a 'mentions' variable which counted the number of times one of the five candidate was mentioned in a given article. Lastly, we were also interested in changes in campaign dynamics between the first and the second round and created an indicator variable to distinguish between the two.

An 11-topic model proved the optimal choice for our analytical purposes. The choice of model involves various iterations of checking the content of the media articles per topic and assigning topic labels by coders. The 11-topic model yielded two broad types of topics. The first grouping comprised six topics and is less important to our inquiry since it was focused mostly on non-policy issues such as information for voters, discussion of the debate, polling results, etc. Our empirical focus is therefore on the policy specific issue areas that were detected in the topic modelling. Five policy themes clearly emerged from our corpus and these were assigned the short labels shown in Figure 3. Figure 3 also shows the estimated topic distribution of our corpus.

The policy topics identified in Figure 3 would be immediately interpretable to any observer of Cypriot politics. In disaggregated form, they comprise some of the most salient issues that structure political competition. Not surprisingly, we find the 'Cyprus problem' as the number one issue in terms of the attention devoted to this topic in the corpus we analysed. The next two topics, labelled 'Social issues' and 'Banks and Bail-in' can be thought of as relating to the socio-economic dimension of political competition. The topics include issues related to welfare, such as hospitals and health spending, on the one hand, and a bundle of issues associated with the financial crisis (the bail-in) and its aftermath, namely the solvency of banks and non-performing loans. It is also interesting to note that the next pair of issues have a connection too. The 'Turkish Cypriots' label refers to an issue that emerged during the elections in the northern part of the island and an incident involving a news agency that was attacked by Turkish nationalists supporting Erdogan. The incident was largely perceived by the Greek Cypriots as an example of Turkish interference in the north, with the primary effect of curtailing freedom of expression. Receiving equal attention was the issue of the Republic's exploitation of the natural gas resources that have been discovered around the coast of the island. Since the elections, this particular issue has become geopolitically explosive, with Turkey-at the time of writing at least-effectively vetoing any unilateral exploitation of the natural gas by the Republic in the absence of a settlement of the Cyprus problem (or wealth sharing with the north).

The topic breakdown in Figure 3 gives an idea of the saliency attached to the issue areas. We now move on to one of the key advantages of fitting an STM model with contextual variables: simply put, it allows us to estimate the effect of changes in covariate values (e.g. a pro-bizonal versus rejectionist media source) on the probability of a topic being emphasised. We begin with this specific, context variable.

In Figure 4 we can see the estimated effects of changing from one group to another. Essentially, positive coefficients on the x axis of the graph whose 95 per cent confidence interval error bars do not cross the zero line, indicate there is a rejectionist bias in the emphasis of the topic. The inverse is the case for negative coefficients, which indicate that the probizonal sources tend to focus on those particular topics. In Figure 4, we can see a clear differentiation in topic emphasis across the sources. Both the Cyprus problem-albeit by a very small margin-and what we have labelled 'Turkish Cypriots' received significantly more attention in the rejectionist-oriented press. Conversely, issues such as the 'Natural gas' exploitation, 'Banks and the Bail-in' and what we have labelled 'Social issues' were more likely to be emphasised by the pro-bizonal-oriented media sources. Some of these issues are also more leftist topics. We were also able to estimate how topics were related to candidates in the first round. The variable we created measured the number of candidate mentions in a given article. It allows us to probe associations between the mention of candidates and specific topics. We can get an overall idea of the media attention devoted to the five candidates in our corpus in Figures 5 and 6. Note the analysis of mentions only pertains to the first round in Figures 5 and 6. It is perhaps not surprising that the incumbent candidate, Anastasiades, received the most attention as shown in Figure 5. What is perhaps more interesting is the distribution among the remaining candidates. It seems that Papadopoulos received somewhat more attention than Malas, even though it was the latter that made it to the second round. Similarly, the mentions of Christou were significantly lower than Lillikaseven though the former received double Lillikas' voter share.

In Figure 6 we group the mentions of the individual candidates by topic uncovered by the STM. There are probably few surprises here, with Anastasiades and Christou mostly associated with the Cyprus problem across the five policy themes. The most surprising finding in Figure 6 is that Papadopoulos was more widely mentioned in connection with the 'Banks and the Bail-in' than with the 'Cyprus problem'. Less surprising is the attention in the first round to 'Social issues' for Malas.

Turning to the second round, our focus now is on the two candidates who contested it. In Figure 7 we compare candidate mentions across the policy areas. A positive coefficient indicates Anastasiades is more highly mentioned compared to Malas while a negative one implies the opposite. Figure 7 shows that Anastasiades is more likely to be mentioned in connection with the 'Banks and Bail-in' topic as well as the 'Natural gas' issue and very marginally more so in relation to the 'Cyprus problem'. That this difference exists for the first two issue areas is not surprising since Anastasiades was the President at the time of the bail-in crisis and is the principal geopolitical interlocutor in relation to the exploitation of the natural gas. On the other hand, there appeared to be no significant differences between the two candidates in relation to the 'Turkish Cypriots' issue. When it came to 'Social issues', the latter topic was most likely to be associated with the leftist candidate, Malas, compared to the incumbent. Note that we do not say anything about whether the candidates are mentioned in a positive or negative light in relation to a particular topic-our focus is simply on who is mentioned the most. While the findings relating to candidate mentions across topics may not be surprising, the fact that they are broadly in line with what one would expect give us greater confidence in the results yielded by the STM. Our last estimate of effects concerns differences between the two electoral rounds. Our interest here was in examining the degree to which there was a differential focus on specific issue areas between the first and the second round. Figure 8 shows that there were indeed significant differences. There is one clear result: the emphasis given to the Cyprus problem was proportionately far greater during the first round than in the second round. Among the probable reasons for this differential was the strong mobilisation on the Cyprus issue of the other significant presidential contender, Nikolas Papadopoulos. His focus on the Cyprus problem was echoed by the other candidates, such as ELAM's candidate, Christos Christou, and former presidential candidate, Giorgos Lillikas. In differentiating themselves as taking a 'harder' line on the Cyprus issue, the three candidates who ultimately did not make it to the second round tried to portray the incumbent and Malas as having the same 'soft' line on the Cyprus problem.

Thus, the perception that the two second round candidates were not so far apart on the Cyprus problem-even if Malas' position was more accommodating towards the Turkish Cypriot community-could be a partial explanation for the reduced focus on this issue in the second round. Meanwhile, the reduced emphasis on the 'Turkish Cypriots' topic, also visible in Figure 8, is connected to the issue fading away by the time of the second round. It is interesting to note that the 'Banks and Bail-in' topic received relatively less attention in round 1 as shown in Figure 8. This could be connected to the mainstream media choosing to focus on aspects related to competence in managing the economy, which also forms part of this topic.

In sum, the reduced 'relative' attention paid in the second round to the super-issue in Cyprus politics-the Cyprus problem-is probably a combination of the portrayal of the two second round candidates as not being so differentiated and the shift in focus to issues related to the economy, and its management. Such a 'competence' framing would obviously benefit the incumbent presidential candidate rather than Malas, who had limited government experience. Nonetheless, taking the two rounds together, the topic modelling does reveal a consistent picture in terms of clustering of specific issue areas around two dominant dimensions that are known to structure the political space in European political systems. As mentioned at the beginning, these are an economic left/ right axis and a cultural dimension of conflict which in Cyprus centres primarily around issues related to the Cyprus problem.

Figure 8 shows that there were indeed significant differences. There is one clear result: the emphasis given to the Cyprus problem was proportionately far greater during the first round than in the second round. Among the probable reasons for this differential was the strong mobilisation on the Cyprus issue of the other significant presidential contender, Nikolas Papadopoulos. His focus on the Cyprus problem was echoed by the other candidates, such as ELAM's candidate, Christos Christou, and former presidential candidate, Giorgos Lillikas. In differentiating themselves as taking a 'harder' line on the Cyprus issue, the three candidates who ultimately did not make it to the second round tried to portray the incumbent and Malas as having the same 'soft' line on the Cyprus problem.

Thus, the perception that the two second round candidates were not so far apart on the Cyprus problem-even if Malas' position was more accommodating towards the Turkish Cypriot community-could be a partial explanation for the reduced focus on this issue in the second round. Meanwhile, the reduced emphasis on the 'Turkish Cypriots' topic, also visible in Figure 8, is connected to the issue fading away by the time of the second round. It is interesting to note that the 'Banks and Bail-in' topic received relatively less attention in round 1 as shown in Figure 8. This could be connected to the mainstream media choosing to focus on aspects related to competence in managing the economy, which also forms part of this topic.

In sum, the reduced 'relative' attention paid in the second round to the super-issue in Cyprus politics-the Cyprus problem-is probably a combination of the portrayal of the two second round candidates as not being so differentiated and the shift in focus to issues related to the economy, and its management. Such a 'competence' framing would obviously benefit the incumbent presidential candidate rather than Malas, who had limited government experience. Nonetheless, taking the two rounds together, the topic modelling does reveal a consistent picture in terms of clustering of specific issue areas around two dominant dimensions that are known to structure the political space in European political systems. As mentioned at the beginning, these are an economic left/ right axis and a cultural dimension of conflict which in Cyprus centres primarily around issues related to the Cyprus problem.
sections.preview_head: We now turn to the core empirical section in this paper: an analysis of how the media reflected the campaign and in particular how different policy issue areas clustered and the relative emphasis given to the main issues. This is connected to our broader concerns in situating the dimensions of political conflict in Cyprus against a wider backdrop of post-crisis politics in European states. To achieve our empirical goals, we implement a topic-modelling approach, a technique used in computer science that has also gained some traction among political scientists for analysing political text (Mildenberger & Tingley 2017;Lucas et al. 2015;Grimmer & Stewart 2013).

paragraphs: Table 3 presents a summary of the results for the two electoral rounds as well as data on the previous election of 2013. The incumbent, Anastasiades, was the clear winner of the two rounds in both 2013 and 2018. Indeed, as can be seen in Table 3, the 2018 results closely mirrored those of the previous presidential elections in 2013 but with a small ±1.5 per cent difference for the second round.

Based on opinion polling throughout the campaign period, none of the candidates was expected to secure a win in the first round. Hence the central question was which two contenders were most likely to qualify for the second round. It is illustrative to look at the evolution of the opinion polls. In Figure 9 we present the aggregated polls for the five candidates as captured by the 23 opinion poll studies published from July 2017 until seven days before the elections were held (see Annex I, at the end of the article). We observe three distinct clusters, with Anastasiades maintaining a stable lead from the beginning until the end of the campaign. In the second cluster, Papadopoulos and Malas are battling for second place. For most of the campaign Papadopoulos enjoyed a lead over Malas, albeit a reduced one by the end. Finally, we can observe the third cluster where Lillikas' initial lead progressively fades away while the candidate of the far right gains steadily over the course of the campaign. In short, the big battle in the first round was which of the two candidates, Papadopoulos or Malas, would make it to the second round.

In the end, it turns out the polls were not so far off with regard to the winner of the first round: Anastasiades came first with 35.5 per cent of the vote. What is notable here, however, is the attrition in Anastasiades' vote share compared to 2013, when he received 45.5 per cent in the first round. Arguably, the most surprising first round 'win' was that of Malas. The small difference of 5.5 per cent between the two lead In 2018, these were the following: Andreas Efstratiou (0.22 per cent), Charis Aristidou (0.19 per cent), Michail Mina (0.17 per cent), Christakis Kapiliotis (0.10 per cent).

candidates-especially compared to 2013 when the difference had been much largercreated some hope for a close second round run-off. The result was received with euphoria by AKEL, the party supporting Malas. The big first round loser was undoubtedly Papadopoulos. As shown in Figure 9, he held a marginal lead in the opinion polls over Malas throughout the campaign. However, his actual vote share was almost six percentage points less than the parties supporting him had polled in the 2016 elections. The electorate, it seems, did not buy into his harder-line approach on the Cyprus question-or at least, not in sufficient numbers. Papadopoulos' failure to make it to the second round also triggered an internal party crisis. Papadopoulos turned out to be a polarising personality, not only for the broader electorate but also for his own party. The electoral defeat did not only damage him personally but also his party, DIKO, which entered another vicious cycle of internal fighting.

Of the remaining cluster, Lillikas and Christou, the former is the biggest loser. Lillikas saw his electoral support crumble from the respectable 25 per cent he had gained in the 2013 election, to barely 2 per cent in 2018. On the other hand, the far right party enjoyed the biggest proportionate gains. In 2013, under a different leader, the party's candidate had gained less than 1 per cent of the vote. In 2018, under the Christou candidacy, this was multiplied by a factor of more than five. Indeed, Christou's electoral result is in line with the steady rise of far right parties across Europe and illustrates how the financial crisis has helped these extreme political forces to capitalise on public discontent (Hernández & Kriesi 2016;Katsourides 2013).

Turning to the second round, the most noteworthy feature was the absence of coalition building that is usually a characteristic of presidential elections in Cyprus. The biggest stumbling block was the position of the two second round candidates on the Cyprus problem. Despite their differences, both Anastasiades and Malas support, at least in principle, a pro-bizonal, bi-communal arrangement. This is precisely the position the other three candidates reject. In an unprecedented situation, none of the three losing candidates endorsed the two second round run-off candidates.

The final election result saw Anastasiades in first place with an eight percentage point difference with Malas. Nevertheless, despite his failure to be elected President, Malas received an unexpectedly high vote share of just over 44 per cent. In this sense, he brought back hope to the second largest Cypriot political party, AKEL, which had suffered electoral setbacks since the end of the Christofias presidency in 2013. One interpretation of the election result is that voters rejected the hard line position on the Cyprus problem, as represented by the three losing candidates in the first round.

A final comment on the election results is warranted with regard to turnout. Despite the fact that the presidential elections are the most salient electoral event, we note a substantial raise in the levels of abstention in both rounds. Abstention in the second round was slightly lower (26 per cent) than the first round (28 per cent) but it was higher by 10 per cent compared to the 2013 elections. In addition, there was a high number of young voters (around 30,000) who did not even bother to register on the electoral lists (Katsourides 2018). This rising trend is no doubt associated with a generalised mistrust of political institutions and with political alienation and cynicism triggered by unaccountability and scandals. This is also consistent with broader dynamics across the EU and can be seen as a side-effect of the Great Recession (Tuorto & Blais 2014;Galais & Blais 2014;Bermeo & Bartels 2014).

sections.paper_id: Triga et al. - 2019 - Post-crisis Political Normalisation The 2018 Presidential Elections in the Republic of Cyprus
paragraphs: Overall, the 2018 presidential elections in Cyprus highlights a return to normality after the turbulence of the financial crisis. The crisis has no doubt left some scars, as is common in many European countries hit by the Great Recession. However, despite being forced to accept an EU/IMF bailout, the incumbent managed to be re-elected. Additionally, this was achieved with a negligible loss in vote share. Among the main reasons for this outcome was the fact that responsibility for the financial crisis was widely attributed to the economic mismanagement of the previous Christofias presidency in 2008-13. By the time of the 2018 elections, the economy was on a clear upward growth trajectory and Anastasiades was able to portray the 'successful' implementation of the rescue package and exit from Troika supervision as the result of his administration's economic competence.

Given such background conditions, the second round of the 2018 presidential election was a re-run of the 2013 contest with virtually similar results. As in 2013, AKEL did not contest the election with a party cadre but backed the same 'independent' candidate, albeit one who was closely linked to the party. This is where the similarities end, however. The 2018 elections were marked by a distinctive climate of political polarisation and shifting issue areas. Relatively side-lined as an issue in the 2013 elections, the Cyprus problem reassumed its central place in structuring political competition. However, as our media analysis revealed, attention to this issue was disproportionately centred on the first round while in the second round the emphasis shifted to economic issues-to the probable advantage of the incumbent.

Overall, then, we find a rather balanced picture in terms of the attention paid to issues reflected in the two dominant axes of political competition in Cyprus: the economic left/right axis on the one hand and the cultural axis (i.e. the Cyprus problem) on the other. While the Cyprus issue regained its pre-eminent role, issues related to the economy and social welfare were notably prominent. And, in this connection, the most salient topic to emerge on the economic front is a direct legacy of the crisis-the banks and non-performing loans.

Other potential legacies of the crisis that may have more enduring, structural effects are also detectable. These include a growing climate of distrust of political elites that has been accompanied by unprecedented levels of abstention. At the same time, there is growing political fragmentation as revealed by more candidates securing significant vote shares-a trend mirrored by the growth in the number of parties represented in parliament. Another point concerns the significant electoral gains made by the far right, which now appears to be less and less a transient political phenomenon. These are all common after-shocks from the financial crisis that have also afflicted many other European countries.

More specific to the Cypriot political regime, was the unprecedented lack of coalition alliances during the run-off stage. Whether this inability to forge bargains and trade-offs is a harbinger of more turbulent times ahead with greater friction between the legislature and the executive remains to be seen. Among the issues that will test the second Anastasiades presidency (a prominent theme identified in the topic modelling) is the issue of natural gas and Turkey's thus far successful attempts to frustrate the exploitation of this resource by the RoC. In a way, this single issue fuses the economic and the cultural while injecting another layer of uncertainty into any forthcoming initiatives on the Cyprus problem. How to navigate these uncharted waters will constitute a formidable challenge for the newly re-elected President.
